name: Picking a date library with unstated constraints
description: Stress pattern: implicit preferences/constraints. Coding. The user never says 'my constraint is' but implies (a) a hard bundle-size limit the team enforces, (b) a preference for zero new runtime dependencies, and (c) TypeScript-first. Tests whether extraction surfaces implicit preferences/constraints.

conversation.content: I need to do some date math in our web app — parsing, adding days, formatting in the user's timezone. People keep suggesting moment.js. Thoughts?

moment.js works but it's in maintenance mode and it's large and mutable. Modern options: date-fns (tree-shakeable functions), Day.js (tiny, moment-like API), Luxon (great timezone support), or the built-in Intl.DateTimeFormat plus Temporal where available. What matters most for you — size, timezone handling, or API ergonomics?
Our marketing site lead bounces any PR that adds more than 10KB gzipped to the main bundle, so moment is a non-starter — it's huge.

Right, moment is ~70KB+ and not tree-shakeable, so it'd blow that budget instantly. date-fns or Day.js fit far better — Day.js is ~2KB core, date-fns lets you import only the functions you use. Both keep you well under 10KB if you're careful with what you pull in.
Timezone formatting is the part I actually care about. We show timestamps in the viewer's local zone.

For viewer-local formatting you may not need a library at all — Intl.DateTimeFormat with the user's resolved timezone handles localized formatting natively, zero bytes added. Libraries earn their keep more for cross-timezone conversion and arithmetic than for 'format in local zone.'
Oh interesting. I'd honestly rather not pull in another package if the platform can do it — every dependency is something the team has to keep patched.

Then lean on built-ins: Intl.DateTimeFormat for formatting, and the Date object (or Temporal where your targets support it) for the add-days arithmetic. That adds nothing to the bundle and nothing to your dependency surface. The one caveat is DST-safe arithmetic, which native Date handles awkwardly.
How bad is the DST issue in practice? Our adds are usually 'add N days to a date' for things like trial expiry.

For 'add N days' on UTC-stored timestamps it's fine — do the arithmetic in UTC and only convert to local at display time. DST bites you mainly when you add days to a local wall-clock time and expect the wall-clock to stay fixed. If your trial dates are UTC under the hood, you're safe with native Date.
They are UTC in the DB. And it'd need to be typed cleanly — half-typed helpers drive me up the wall in our codebase.

Then write a small, fully-typed utils/date.ts: functions like addDays(date: Date, n: number): Date and formatLocal(date: Date, tz: string): string wrapping Intl. You get strict types, zero dependencies, no bundle cost, and DST-safe behavior since you operate in UTC. Want me to sketch that module?
Yes please, sketch addDays and formatLocal with proper types.
I'll write a typed utils/date.ts with addDays(date, n) doing UTC-based arithmetic and formatLocal(date, timeZone) using Intl.DateTimeFormat, both fully typed with no external imports.
